facts.text: Thorofare earned its name from the early colonial crossroads where several main roads met, making it a key travel hub.
By the late 1800s Thorofare hosted a Methodist Episcopal Church, a schoolhouse that hosted the first town meeting, a railroad station, and about twenty homes.
Thorofare sits on the western edge of West Deptford, right on the Delaware River that marks the New Jersey‑Pennsylvania line, just 10 miles south of Philadelphia.
The area enjoys a humid subtropical climate, with about 45 inches of rain yearly and winter lows near 25°F and summer highs around 85°F.
Thorofare’s elevation averages only about 20 feet above sea level, keeping it close to the river’s floodplain.
Dutch explorers first claimed the area with Fort Nassau in 1623, but the English seized it in 1664, later granting it to Quaker proprietors in 1674.
